title: Organisms and Populations
subtitle: Abiotic factors, adaptations, population attributes, growth models and species interactions - ecology made interactive and exam-ready.

heroModel.title: Population growth model
heroModel.subtitle: Drag to rotate - scroll to zoom

roadmapTitle: Ecology begins with organisms responding to their environment.
roadmapDescription: Move from abiotic factors to adaptations, then population growth and interactions.
flowchart:
flowchart LR
  A[Organism] --> B[Abiotic factors]
  B --> C[Adaptations]
  C --> D[Population attributes]
  D --> E[Exponential growth]
  D --> F[Logistic growth]
  E --> G[Interactions]
  F --> G
  G --> H[Niche and community effects]

roadmap.title: Organism and environment
roadmap.summary: Temperature, water, light and soil shape survival.

roadmap.title: Population attributes
roadmap.summary: Density, birth rate, death rate, sex ratio and age distribution.

roadmap.title: Population interactions
roadmap.summary: Competition, predation, parasitism, mutualism, commensalism and amensalism.

roadmap.title: Niche
roadmap.summary: Functional role and resource use pattern of an organism.

conceptTitle: Population ecology as graphs and interactions.
conceptDescription: These cards convert theory into curves, symbols and relationship logic.

sections.title: Major abiotic factors
sections.description: Temperature, water, light and soil influence organism distribution and survival.
points: Temperature affects enzyme activity and metabolism.
Water availability is critical for terrestrial organisms.
Light affects photosynthesis and photoperiodic responses.
Soil properties affect plants and decomposers.
sections.neet: Temperature is the most ecologically relevant environmental factor in many contexts.

sections.title: Regulate, conform, migrate, suspend
sections.description: Organisms respond to environmental change in different ways.
points: Regulators maintain internal homeostasis.
Conformers allow body conditions to vary with environment.
Migration avoids stressful conditions.
Suspension includes hibernation, aestivation, diapause and dormancy.
sections.neet: Most animals and plants are conformers, not regulators.

sections.title: Adaptations
sections.description: Adaptations improve survival and reproduction in a habitat.
points: Kangaroo rat conserves water in desert.
Mammals in cold areas may have thick fur and fat.
Altitude adaptation includes increased RBC production.
Behavioural adaptations include basking or burrowing.
sections.neet: Kangaroo rat is a classic desert adaptation example.

sections.title: Population attributes
sections.description: Population has properties that individuals do not.
points: Population density measures number per unit area/volume.
Natality adds individuals.
Mortality removes individuals.
Age pyramids indicate growing, stable or declining population.
sections.neet: Birth rate and death rate are population-level attributes.

sections.title: Exponential and logistic growth
sections.description: Population growth depends on resources and environmental resistance.
points: Exponential growth occurs with unlimited resources.
Logistic growth occurs with limited resources.
K is carrying capacity.
Logistic curve is sigmoid.
sections.neet: Logistic growth is more realistic in nature.

sections.title: Population interactions
sections.description: Species interactions are classified by benefit or harm.
points: Mutualism benefits both species.
Competition harms both by resource limitation.
Predation benefits predator and harms prey.
Commensalism benefits one and does not affect the other.
sections.neet: +/+ is mutualism; +/- is predation or parasitism.

sections.title: Competition and resource partitioning
sections.description: Competition occurs when species share limiting resources.
points: Gause principle: two closely related species cannot coexist indefinitely on same limiting resource.
Resource partitioning reduces competition.
Competition can be interspecific or intraspecific.
Interference and exploitation are forms of competition.
sections.neet: Competitive exclusion is linked with Gause principle.

sections.title: Predation, parasitism and defence
sections.description: Predators and parasites influence population size and adaptations.
points: Predators transfer energy through food chains.
Prey evolve camouflage, toxins or spines.
Parasites depend on hosts and may reduce fitness.
Brood parasitism is seen in cuckoo example.
sections.neet: Predation can maintain species diversity by controlling dominant prey.

modelDescription: Rotate growth curves, interaction networks and niche models.
highYield: Major abiotic factors are temperature, water, light and soil.
Suspension includes hibernation, aestivation, diapause and dormancy.
Population density, natality, mortality, sex ratio and age distribution are population attributes.

mistakes.title: Individual vs population attribute
mistakes.body: Birth rate and death rate apply to populations, not single organisms.

mistakes.title: Exponential vs logistic
mistakes.body: Exponential assumes unlimited resources; logistic includes carrying capacity.

mistakes.title: Predation vs parasitism
mistakes.body: Both are +/-, but parasite usually lives in/on host and may not kill immediately.

quiz.question: Logistic growth curve is:
quiz.explain: Limited resources produce sigmoid logistic curve.

quiz.question: Most organisms are:
quiz.explain: Most animals and plants cannot maintain constant internal conditions always.

quiz.question: Gause principle relates to:
quiz.explain: Two species competing for same limiting resource cannot coexist indefinitely.

quiz.question: Kangaroo rat is an example of:

revisionTable: Density, natality, mortality, age, sex ratio
